Your lordships, that, whenever you have need,
You may be armed and appointed well:
And so I leave you both:
[Aside]
like bloody villains.
Exeunt Young LUCIUS, and Attendant

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
What's here? A scroll; and written round about?
Let's see;
[Reads]
'Integer vitae, scelerisque purus,
Non eget Mauri jaculis, nec arcu.'

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text:
O, 'tis a verse in Horace; I know it well:
I read it in the grammar long ago.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Ay, just; a verse in Horace; right, you have it.
[Aside]
Now, what a thing it is to be an ass!
Here's no sound jest! the old man hath found their
guilt;
And sends them weapons wrapped about with lines,
That wound, beyond their feeling, to the quick.
But were our witty empress well afoot,
She would applaud Andronicus' conceit:
But let her rest in her unrest awhile.
And now, young lords, was't not a happy star
Led us to Rome, strangers, and more than so,
Captives, to be advanced to this height?
It did me good, before the palace gate
To brave the tribune in his brother's hearing.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
But me more good, to see so great a lord
Basely insinuate and send us gifts.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Had he not reason, Lord Demetrius?
Did you not use his daughter very friendly?

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
I would we had a thousand Roman dames
At such a bay, by turn to serve our lust.

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: A charitable wish and full of love.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Here lacks but your mother for to say amen.

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: And that would she for twenty thousand more.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
Come, let us go; and pray to all the gods
For our beloved mother in her pains.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
[Aside] Pray to the devils; the gods have given us
over.
Trumpets sound within

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: Why do the emperor's trumpets flourish thus?

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: Belike, for joy the emperor hath a son.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: Soft! who comes here?
Enter a Nurse, with a blackamoor Child in her arms

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text:
Good morr ow, lords:
O, tell me, did you see Aaron the Moor?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Well, more or less, or ne'er a whit at all,
Here Aaron is; and what with Aaron now?

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text:
O gentle Aaron, we are all undone!
Now help, or woe betide thee evermore!

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Why, what a caterwauling dost thou keep!
What dost thou wrap and fumble in thine arms?

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text:
O, that which I would hide from heaven's eye,
Our empress' shame, and stately Rome's disgrace!
She is deliver'd, lords; she is deliver'd.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: To whom?

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text: I mean, she is brought a-bed.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Well, God give her good rest! What hath he sent her?

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text: A devil.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Why, then she is the devil's dam; a joyful issue.

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text:
A joyless, dismal, black, and sorrowful issue:
Here is the babe, as loathsome as a toad
Amongst the fairest breeders of our clime:
The empress sends it thee, thy stamp, thy seal,
And bids thee christen it with thy dagger's point.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
'Zounds, ye whore! is black so base a hue?
Sweet blowse, you are a beauteous blossom, sure.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: Villain, what hast thou done?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: That which thou canst not undo.

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: Thou hast undone our mother.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Villain, I have done thy mother.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
And therein, hellish dog, thou hast undone.
Woe to her chance, and damn'd her loathed choice!
Accursed the offspring of so foul a fiend!

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: It shall not live.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: It shall not die.

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text: Aaron, it must; the mother wills it so.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
What, must it, nurse? then let no man but I
Do execution on my flesh and blood.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
I'll broach the tadpole on my rapier's point:
Nurse, give it me; my sword shall soon dispatch it.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Sooner this sword shall plough thy bowels up.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: Wilt thou betray thy noble mistress thus?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
My mistress is my mistress; this myself,
The vigour and the picture of my youth:
This before all the world do I prefer;
This maugre all the world will I keep safe,
Or some of you shall smoke for it in Rome.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: By this our mother is forever shamed.

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: Rome will despise her for this foul escape.

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text: The emperor, in his rage, will doom her death.

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: I blush to think upon this ignomy.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Why, there's the privilege your beauty bears:
Fie, treacherous hue, that will betray with blushing
The close enacts and counsels of the heart!
Here's a young lad framed of another leer:
Look, how the black slave smiles upon the father,
As who should say 'Old lad, I am thine own.'
He is your brother, lords, sensibly fed
Of that self-blood that first gave life to you,
And from that womb where you imprison'd were
He is enfranchised and come to light:
Nay, he is your brother by the surer side,
Although my seal be stamped in his face.

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text: Aaron, what shall I say unto the empress?

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
Advise thee, Aaron, what is to be done,
And we will all subscribe to thy advice:
Save thou the child, so we may all be safe.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Then sit we down, and let us all consult.
My son and I will have the wind of you:
Keep there: now talk at pleasure of your safety.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: How many women saw this child of his?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Why, so, brave lords! when we join in league,
I am a lamb: but if you brave the Moor,
The chafed boar, the mountain lioness,
The ocean swells not so as Aaron storms.
But say, again; how many saw the child?

units.speaker: NURSE
units.text:
Cornelia the midwife and myself;
And no one else but the deliver'd empress.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
The empress, the midwife, and yourself:
Two may keep counsel when the third's away:
Go to the empress, tell her this I said.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: What mean'st thou, Aaron? wherefore didst thou this?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
O Lord, sir, 'tis a deed of policy:
Shall she live to betray this guilt of ours,
A long-tongued babbling gossip? no, lords, no:
And now be it known to you my full intent.
Not far, one Muli lives, my countryman;
His wife but yesternight was brought to bed;
His child is like to her, fair as you are:
Go pack with him, and give the mother gold,
And tell them both the circumstance of all;
And how by this their child shall be advanced,
And be received for the emperor's heir,
And substituted in the place of mine,
To calm this tempest whirling in the court;
And let the emperor dandle him for his own.
Hark ye, lords; ye see I have given her physic,

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text:
Aaron, I see thou wilt not trust the air
With secrets.

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text:
For this care of Tamora,
Herself and hers are highly bound to thee.
Exeunt DEMETRIUS and CHIRON bearing off the Nurse's body

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Now to the Goths, as swift as swallow flies;
There to dispose this treasure in mine arms,
And secretly to greet the empress' friends.
Come on, you thick lipp'd slave, I'll bear you hence;
For it is you that puts us to our shifts:
I'll make you feed on berries and on roots,
And feed on curds and whey, and suck the goat,
And cabin in a cave, and bring you up
To be a warrior, and command a camp.
Exit
Enter TITUS, bearing arrows with letters at the ends of them; with him, MARCUS, Young LUCIUS, PUBLIUS, SEMPRONIUS, CAIUS, and other Gentlemen, with bows

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Come, Marcus; come, kinsmen; this is the way.
Sir boy, now let me see your archery;
Look ye draw home enough, and 'tis there straight.
Terras Astraea reliquit:
Be you remember'd, Marcus, she's gone, she's fled.
Sirs, take you to your tools. You, cousins, shall
Go sound the ocean, and cast your nets;
Happily you may catch her in the sea;
Yet there's as little justice as at land:
No; Publius and Sempronius, you must do it;
'Tis you must dig with mattock and with spade,
And pierce the inmost centre of the earth:
Then, when you come to Pluto's region,
I pray you, deliver him this petition;
Tell him, it is for justice and for aid,
And that it comes from old Andronicus,
Shaken with sorrows in ungrateful Rome.
Ah, Rome! Well, well; I made thee miserable
What time I threw the people's suffrages
On him that thus doth tyrannize o'er me.
Go, get you gone; and pray be careful all,
And leave you not a man-of-war unsearch'd:
This wicked emperor may have shipp'd her hence;
And, kinsmen, then we may go pipe for justice.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
O Publius, is not this a heavy case,
To see thy noble uncle thus distract?

units.speaker: PUBLIUS
units.text:
Therefore, my lord, it highly us concerns
By day and night to attend him carefully,
And feed his humour kindly as we may,
Till time beget some careful remedy.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Kinsmen, his sorrows are past remedy.
Join with the Goths; and with revengeful war
Take wreak on Rome for this ingratitude,
And vengeance on the traitor Saturnine.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Publius, how now! how now, my masters!
What, have you met with her?

units.speaker: PUBLIUS
units.text:
No, my good lord; but Pluto sends you word,
If you will have Revenge from hell, you shall:
Marry, for Justice, she is so employ'd,
He thinks, with Jove in heaven, or somewhere else,
So that perforce you must needs stay a time.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
He doth me wrong to feed me with delays.
I'll dive into the burning lake below,
And pull her out of Acheron by the heels.
Marcus, we are but shrubs, no cedars we
No big-boned men framed of the Cyclops' size;
But metal, Marcus, steel to the very back,
Yet wrung with wrongs more than our backs can bear:
And, sith there's no justice in earth nor hell,
We will solicit heaven and move the gods
To send down Justice for to wreak our wrongs.
Come, to this gear. You are a good archer, Marcus;

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Kinsmen, shoot all your shafts into the court:
We will afflict the emperor in his pride.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Now, masters, draw.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
My lord, I aim a mile beyond the moon;
Your letter is with Jupiter by this.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Ha, ha!
Publius, Publius, what hast thou done?
See, see, thou hast shot off one of Taurus' horns.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
This was the sport, my lord: when Publius shot,
The Bull, being gall'd, gave Aries such a knock
That down fell both the Ram's horns in the court;
And who should find them but the empress' villain?
She laugh'd, and told the Moor he should not choose
But give them to his master for a present.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Why, there it goes: God give his lordship joy!
Enter a Clown, with a basket, and two pigeons in it

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
News, news from heaven! Marcus, the post is come.
Sirrah, what tidings? have you any letters?
Shall I have justice? what says Jupiter?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text:
O, the gibbet-maker! he says that he hath taken
them down again, for the man must not be hanged till
the next week.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: But what says Jupiter, I ask thee?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text:
Alas, sir, I know not Jupiter; I never drank with him
in all my life.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Why, villain, art not thou the carrier?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text: Ay, of my pigeons, sir; nothing else.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Why, didst thou not come from heaven?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text:
From heaven! alas, sir, I never came there God
forbid I should be so bold to press to heaven in my
young days. Why, I am going with my pigeons to the
tribunal plebs, to take up a matter of brawl
betwixt my uncle and one of the emperial's men.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Why, sir, that is as fit as can be to serve for
your oration; and let him deliver the pigeons to
the emperor from you.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Tell me, can you deliver an oration to the emperor
with a grace?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text:
Nay, truly, sir, I could never say grace in all my
life.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Sirrah, come hither: make no more ado,
But give your pigeons to the emperor:
By me thou shalt have justice at his hands.
Hold, hold; meanwhile here's money for thy charges.
Give me pen and ink. Sirrah, can you with a grace
deliver a supplication?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text: Ay, sir.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Then here is a supplication for you. And when you
come to him, at the first approach you must kneel,
then kiss his foot, then deliver up your pigeons, and
then look for your reward. I'll be at hand, sir; see
you do it bravely.

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text: I warrant you, sir, let me alone.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Sirrah, hast thou a knife? come, let me see it.
Here, Marcus, fold it in the oration;
For thou hast made it like an humble suppliant.
And when thou hast given it the emperor,
Knock at my door, and tell me what he says.

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text: God be with you, sir; I will.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Come, Marcus, let us go. Publius, follow me.
Exeunt
Enter SATURNINUS, TAMORA, DEMETRIUS, CHIRON, Lords, and others; SATURNINUS with the arrows in his hand that TITUS shot

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text:
Why, lords, what wrongs are these! was ever seen
An emperor in Rome thus overborne,
Troubled, confronted thus; and, for the extent
Of equal justice, used in such contempt?
My lords, you know, as know the mightful gods,
However these disturbers of our peace
Buzz in the people's ears, there nought hath pass'd,
But even with law, against the willful sons
Of old Andronicus. And what an if
His sorrows have so overwhelm'd his wits,
Shall we be thus afflicted in his wreaks,
His fits, his frenzy, and his bitterness?
And now he writes to heaven for his redress:
See, here's to Jove, and this to Mercury;
This to Apollo; this to the god of war;
Sweet scrolls to fly about the streets of Rome!
What's this but libelling against the senate,
And blazoning our injustice every where?
A goodly humour, is it not, my lords?
As who would say, in Rome no justice were.
But if I live, his feigned ecstasies
Shall be no shelter to these outrages:
But he and his shall know that justice lives
In Saturninus' health, whom, if she sleep,
He'll so awake as she in fury shall
Cut off the proud'st conspirator that lives.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
My gracious lord, my lovely Saturnine,
Lord of my life, commander of my thoughts,
Calm thee, and bear the faults of Titus' age,
The effects of sorrow for his valiant sons,
Whose loss hath pierced him deep and scarr'd his heart;
And rather comfort his distressed plight
Than prosecute the meanest or the best
For these contempts.
[Aside]
Why, thus it shall become
High-witted Tamora to gloze with all:
But, Titus, I have touched thee to the quick,
Thy life-blood out: if Aaron now be wise,
Then is all safe, the anchor's in the port.
Enter Clown

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: How now, good fellow! wouldst thou speak with us?

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text: Yea, forsooth, an your mistership be emperial.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: Empress I am, but yonder sits the emperor.

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text:
'Tis he. God and Saint Stephen give you good den:
I have brought you a letter and a couple of pigeons
here.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: Go, take him away, and hang him presently.

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text: How much money must I have?

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: Come, sirrah, you must be hanged.

units.speaker: CLOWN
units.text:
Hanged! by'r lady, then I have brought up a neck to
a fair end.
Exit, guarded

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text:
Despiteful and intolerable wrongs!
Shall I endure this monstrous villany?
I know from whence this same device proceeds:
May this be borne?— as if his traitorous sons,
That died by law for murder of our brother,
Have by my means been butcher'd wrongfully!
Go, drag the villain hither by the hair;
Nor age nor honour shall shape privilege:
For this proud mock I'll be thy slaughterman;
Sly frantic wretch, that holp'st to make me great,
In hope thyself should govern Rome and me.
Enter AEMILIUS

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: What news with thee, AEmilius?

units.speaker: AEMILIUS
units.text:
Arm, arm, my lord;—Rome never had more cause.
The Goths have gather'd head; and with a power
high-resolved men, bent to the spoil,
They hither march amain, under conduct
Of Lucius, son to old Andronicus;
Who threats, in course of this revenge, to do
As much as ever Coriolanus did.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text:
Is warlike Lucius general of the Goths?
These tidings nip me, and I hang the head
As flowers with frost or grass beat down with storms:
Ay, now begin our sorrows to approach:
'Tis he the common people love so much;
Myself hath often over-heard them say,
When I have walked like a private man,
That Lucius' banishment was wrongfully,
And they have wish'd that Lucius were their emperor.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: Why should you fear? is not your city strong?

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text:
Ay, but the citizens favor Lucius,
And will revolt from me to succor him.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
King, be thy thoughts imperious, like thy name.
Is the sun dimm'd, that gnats do fly in it?
The eagle suffers little birds to sing,
And is not careful what they mean thereby,
Knowing that with the shadow of his wings
He can at pleasure stint their melody:
Even so mayst thou the giddy men of Rome.
Then cheer thy spirit : for know, thou emperor,
I will enchant the old Andronicus
With words more sweet, and yet more dangerous,
Than baits to fish, or honey-stalks to sheep,
When as the one is wounded with the bait,
The other rotted with delicious feed.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: But he will not entreat his son for us.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
If Tamora entreat him, then he will:
For I can smooth and fill his aged ear
With golden promises; that, were his heart
Almost impregnable, his old ears deaf,
Yet should both ear and heart obey my tongue.
[To AEMILIUS]
Go thou before, be our ambassador:
Say that the emperor requests a parley
Of warlike Lucius, and appoint the meeting
Even at his father's house, the old Andronicus.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text:
AEmilius, do this message honourably:
And if he stand on hostage for his safety,
Bid him demand what pledge will please him best.

units.speaker: AEMILIUS
units.text: Your bidding shall I do effectually.
Exit

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Now will I to that old Andronicus;
And temper him with all the art I have,
To pluck proud Lucius from the warlike Goths.
And now, sweet emperor, be blithe again,
And bury all thy fear in my devices.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: Then go successantly, and plead to him.
Exeunt
Enter LUCIUS with an army of Goths, with drum and colours

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Approved warriors, and my faithful friends,
I have received letters from great Rome,
Which signify what hate they bear their emperor
And how desirous of our sight they are.
Therefore, great lords, be, as your titles witness,
Imperious and impatient of your wrongs,
And wherein Rome hath done you any scath,
Let him make treble satisfaction.

units.speaker: FIRST GOTH
units.text:
Brave slip, sprung from the great Andronicus,
Whose name was once our terror, now our comfort;
Whose high exploits and honourable deeds
Ingrateful Rome requites with foul contempt,
Be bold in us: we'll follow where thou lead'st,
Like stinging bees in hottest summer's day
Led by their master to the flowered fields,
And be avenged on cursed Tamora.

units.speaker: ALL THE GOTHS
units.text: And as he saith, so say we all with him.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
I humbly thank him, and I thank you all.
But who comes here, led by a lusty Goth?
Enter a Goth, leading AARON with his Child in his arms

units.speaker: SECOND GOTH
units.text:
Renowned Lucius, from our troops I stray'd
To gaze upon a ruinous monastery;
And, as I earnestly did fix mine eye
Upon the wasted building, suddenly
I heard a child cry underneath a wall.
I made unto the noise; when soon I heard
The crying babe controll'd with this discourse:
'Peace, tawny slave, half me and half thy dam!
Did not thy hue bewray whose brat thou art,
Had nature lent thee but thy mother's look,
Villain, thou mightst have been an emperor:
But where the bull and cow are both milk-white,
They never do beget a coal-black calf.
Peace, villain, peace!'—even thus he rates
the babe,—
'For I must bear thee to a trusty Goth;
Who, when he knows thou art the empress' babe,
Will hold thee dearly for thy mother's sake.'
With this, my weapon drawn, I rush'd upon him,
Surprised him suddenly, and brought him hither,
To use as you think needful of the man.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
O worthy Goth, this is the incarnate devil
That robb'd Andronicus of his good hand;
This is the pearl that pleased your empress' eye,
And here's the base fruit of his burning lust.
Say, wall-eyed slave, whither wouldst thou convey
This growing image of thy fiend-like face?
Why dost not speak? what, deaf? not a word?
A halter, soldiers! hang him on this tree.
And by his side his fruit of bastardy.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Touch not the boy; he is of royal blood.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Too like the sire for ever being good.
First hang the child, that he may see it sprawl;
A sight to vex the father's soul withal.
Get me a ladder.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Lucius, save the child,
And bear it from me to the empress.
If thou do this, I'll show thee wondrous things,
That highly may advantage thee to hear:
If thou wilt not, befall what may befall,
I'll speak no more but 'Vengeance rot you all!'

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Say on: an if it please me which thou speak'st
Thy child shall live, and I will see it nourish'd.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
An if it please thee! why, assure thee, Lucius,
'Twill vex thy soul to hear what I shall speak;
For I must talk of murders, rapes and massacres,
Acts of black night, abominable deeds,
Complots of mischief, treason, villanies
Ruthful to hear, yet piteously perform'd:
And this shall all be buried by my death,
Unless thou swear to me my child shall live.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Tell on thy mind; I say thy child shall live.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Swear that he shall, and then I will begin.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Who should I swear by? thou believest no god:
That granted, how canst thou believe an oath?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
What if I do not? as, indeed, I do not;
Yet, for I know thou art religious
And hast a thing within thee called conscience,
With twenty popish tricks and ceremonies,
Which I have seen thee careful to observe,
Therefore I urge thy oath; for that I know
An idiot holds his bauble for a god
And keeps the oath which by that god he swears,
To that I'll urge him: therefore thou shalt vow
By that same god, what god soe'er it be,
That thou adorest and hast in reverence,
To save my boy, to nourish and bring him up;
Or else I will discover nought to thee.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Even by my god I swear to thee I will.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: First know thou, I begot him on the empress.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: O most insatiate and luxurious woman!

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Tut, Lucius, this was but a deed of charity
To that which thou shalt hear of me anon.
'Twas her two sons that murder'd Bassianus;
They cut thy sister's tongue and ravish'd her
And cut her hands and trimm'd her as thou saw'st.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: O detestable villain! call'st thou that trimming?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Why, she was wash'd and cut and trimm'd, and 'twas
Trim sport for them that had the doing of it.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: O barbarous, beastly villains, like thyself!

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Indeed, I was their tutor to instruct them:
That codding spirit had they from their mother,
As sure a card as ever won the set;
That bloody mind, I think, they learn'd of me,
As true a dog as ever fought at head.
Well, let my deeds be witness of my worth.
I train'd thy brethren to that guileful hole
Where the dead corpse of Bassianus lay:
I wrote the letter that thy father found
And hid the gold within the letter mention'd,
Confederate with the queen and her two sons:
And what not done, that thou hast cause to rue,
Wherein I had no stroke of mischief in it?
I play'd the cheater for thy father's hand,
And, when I had it, drew myself apart
And almost broke my heart with extreme laughter:
I pry'd me through the crevice of a wall
When, for his hand, he had his two sons' heads;
Beheld his tears, and laugh'd so heartily,
That both mine eyes were rainy like to his :
And when I told the empress of this sport,
She swooned almost at my pleasing tale,
And for my tidings gave me twenty kisses.

units.speaker: FIRST GOTH
units.text: What, canst thou say all this, and never blush?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text: Ay, like a black dog, as the saying is.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Art thou not sorry for these heinous deeds?

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Ay, that I had not done a thousand more.
Even now I curse the day—and yet, I think,
Few come within the compass of my curse,—
Wherein I did not some notorious ill,
As kill a man, or else devise his death,
Ravish a maid, or plot the way to do it,
Accuse some innocent and forswear myself,
Set deadly enmity between two friends,
Make poor men's cattle break their necks;
Set fire on barns and hay-stacks in the night,
And bid the owners quench them with their tears.
Oft have I digg'd up dead men from their graves,
And set them upright at their dear friends' doors,
Even when their sorrows almost were forgot;
And on their skins, as on the bark of trees,
Have with my knife carved in Roman letters,
'Let not your sorrow die, though I am dead.'
Tut, I have done a thousand dreadful things
As willingly as one would kill a fly,
And nothing grieves me heartily indeed
But that I cannot do ten thousand more.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Bring down the devil; for he must not die
So sweet a death as hanging presently.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
If there be devils, would I were a devil,
To live and burn in everlasting fire,
So I might have your company in hell,
But to torment you with my bitter tongue!

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Sirs, stop his mouth, and let him speak no more.
Enter a Goth

units.speaker: THIRD GOTH
units.text:
My lord, there is a messenger from Rome
Desires to be admitted to your presence.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Let him come near.
Enter AEMILIUS

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: Welcome, AEmilius what's the news from Rome?

units.speaker: AEMILIUS
units.text:
Lord Lucius, and you princes of the Goths,
The Roman emperor greets you all by me;
And, for he understands you are in arms,
He craves a parley at your father's house,
Willing you to demand your hostages,
And they shall be immediately deliver'd.

units.speaker: FIRST GOTH
units.text: What says our general?

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
AEmilius, let the emperor give his pledges
Unto my father and my uncle Marcus,
And we will come. March away.
Exeunt
Enter TAMORA, DEMETRIUS, and CHIRON, disguised

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Thus, in this strange and sad habiliment,
I will encounter with Andronicus,
And say I am Revenge, sent from below
To join with him and right his heinous wrongs.
Knock at his study, where, they say, he keeps,
To ruminate strange plots of dire revenge;
Tell him Revenge is come to join with him,
And work confusion on his enemies.
Enter TITUS, above

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Who doth molest my contemplation?
Is it your trick to make me ope the door,
That so my sad decrees may fly away,
And all my study be to no effect?
You are deceived: for what I mean to do
See here in bloody lines I have set down;
And what is written shall be executed.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: Titus, I am come to talk with thee.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
No, not a word; how can I grace my talk,
Wanting a hand to give it action?
Thou hast the odds of me; therefore no more.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: If thou didst know me, thou wouldest talk with me.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
I am not mad; I know thee well enough:
Witness this wretched stump, witness these crimson
lines;
Witness these trenches made by grief and care,
Witness the tiring day and heavy night;
Witness all sorrow, that I know thee well
For our proud empress, mighty Tamora:
Is not thy coming for my other hand?

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Know, thou sad man, I am not Tamora;
She is thy enemy, and I thy friend:
I am Revenge: sent from the infernal kingdom,
To ease the gnawing vulture of thy mind,
By working wreakful vengeance on thy foes.
Come down, and welcome me to this world's light;
Confer with me of murder and of death:
There's not a hollow cave or lurking-place,
No vast obscurity or misty vale,
Where bloody murder or detested rape
Can couch for fear, but I will find them out;
And in their ears tell them my dreadful name,
Revenge, which makes the foul offender quake.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Art thou Revenge? and art thou sent to me,
To be a torment to mine enemies?

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: I am; therefore come down, and welcome me.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Do me some service, ere I come to thee.
Lo, by thy side where Rape and Murder stands;
Now give me some surance that thou art Revenge,
Stab them, or tear them on thy chariot-wheels;
And then I'll come and be thy waggoner,
And whirl along with thee about the globe.
Provide thee two proper palfreys, black as jet,
To hale thy vengeful waggon swift away,
And find out murderers in their guilty caves:
And when thy car is loaden with their heads,
I will dismount, and by the waggon-wheel
Trot, like a servile footman, all day long,
Even from Hyperion's rising in the east
Until his very downfall in the sea:
And day by day I'll do this heavy task,
So thou destroy Rapine and Murder there.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: These are my ministers, and come with me.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Are these thy ministers? what are they call'd?

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Rapine and Murder; therefore called so,
Cause they take vengeance of such kind of men.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Good Lord, how like the empress' sons they are!
And you, the empress! but we worldly men
Have miserable, mad, mistaking eyes.
O sweet Revenge, now do I come to thee;
And, if one arm's embracement will content thee,
I will embrace thee in it by and by.
Exit above

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
This closing with him fits his lunacy
Whate'er I forge to feed his brain-sick fits,
Do you uphold and maintain in your speeches,
For now he firmly takes me for Revenge;
And, being credulous in this mad thought,
I'll make him send for Lucius his son;
And, whilst I at a banquet hold him sure,
I'll find some cunning practise out of hand,
To scatter and disperse the giddy Goths,
Or, at the least, make them his enemies.
See, here he comes, and I must ply my theme.
Enter TITUS below

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Long have I been forlorn, and all for thee:
Welcome, dread Fury, to my woful house:
Rapine and Murder, you are welcome too.
How like the empress and her sons you are!
Well are you fitted, had you but a Moor:
Could not all hell afford you such a devil?
For well I wot the empress never wags
But in her company there is a Moor;
And, would you represent our queen aright,
It were convenient you had such a devil:
But welcome, as you are. What shall we do?

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: What wouldst thou have us do, Andronicus?

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: Show me a murderer, I'll deal with him.

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text:
Show me a villain that hath done a rape,
And I am sent to be revenged on him.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Show me a thousand that have done thee wrong,
And I will be revenged on them all.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
TAMORA
units.text:
Well hast thou lesson'd us; this shall we do.
But would it please thee, good Andronicus,
To send for Lucius, thy thrice-valiant son,
Who leads towards Rome a band of warlike Goths,
And bid him come and banquet at thy house;
When he is here, even at thy solemn feast,
I will bring in the empress and her sons,
The emperor himself and all thy foes;
And at thy mercy shalt they stoop and kneel,
And on them shalt thou ease thy angry heart.
What says Andronicus to this device?

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Marcus, my brother! 'tis sad Titus calls.
Enter MARCUS

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Go, gentle Marcus, to thy nephew Lucius;
Thou shalt inquire him out among the Goths:
Bid him repair to me, and bring with him
Some of the chiefest princes of the Goths;
Bid him encamp his soldiers where they are:
Tell him the emperor and the empress too
Feast at my house, and he shall feast with them.
This do thou for my love; and so let him,
As he regards his aged father's life.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: This will I do, and soon return again.
Exit

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Now will I hence about thy business,
And take my ministers along with me.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
TAMORA
units.text:
[Aside to her sons] What say you, boys? will you
bide with him,
Whiles I go tell my lord the emperor
How I have govern'd our determined jest?
Yield to his humour, smooth and speak him fair,
And tarry with him till I turn again.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
[Aside] I know them all, though they suppose me mad,
And will o'erreach them in their own devices:
A pair of cursed hell-hounds and their dam!

units.speaker: DEMETRIUS
units.text: Madam, depart at pleasure; leave us here.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text:
Farewell, Andronicus: Revenge now goes
To lay a complot to betray thy foes.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: I know thou dost; and, sweet Revenge, farewell.
Exit TAMORA

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: Tell us, old man, how shall we be employ'd?

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Tut, I have work enough for you to do.
Publius, come hither, Caius, and Valentine!
Enter PUBLIUS and others

units.speaker: PUBLIUS
units.text: What is your will?

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Know you these two?

units.speaker: PUBLIUS
units.text: The empress' sons, I take them, Chiron and Demetrius.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Fie, Publius, fie! thou art too much deceived;
The one is Murder, Rape is the other's name;
And therefore bind them, gentle Publius.
Caius and Valentine, lay hands on them.
Oft have you heard me wish for such an hour,
And now I find it; therefore bind them sure,
And stop their mouths, if they begin to cry.
Exit

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: PUBLIUS, & c. lay hold on CHIRON and DEMETRIUS

units.speaker: CHIRON
units.text: Villains, forbear! we are the empress' sons.

units.speaker: PUBLIUS
units.text:
And therefore do we what we are commanded.
Stop close their mouths, let them not speak a word.
Is he sure bound? look that you bind them fast.
Re-enter TITUS, with LAVINIA; he bearing a knife, and she a basin

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Come, come, Lavinia; look, thy foes are bound.
Sirs, stop their mouths, let them not speak to me;
But let them hear what fearful words I utter.
O villains, Chiron and Demetrius!
Here stands the spring whom you have stain'd with mud,
This goodly summer with your winter mix'd.
You kill'd her husband, and for that vile fault
Two of her brothers were condemn'd to death,
My hand cut off and made a merry jest;
Both her sweet hands, her tongue, and that more dear
Than hands or tongue, her spotless chastity,
Inhuman traitors, you constrain'd and forced.
What would you say, if I should let you speak?
Villains, for shame you could not beg for grace.
Hark, wretches! how I mean to martyr you.
This one hand yet is left to cut your throats,
Whilst that Lavinia 'tween her stumps doth hold
The basin that receives your guilty blood.
You know your mother means to feast with me,
And calls herself Revenge, and thinks me mad:
Hark, villains! I will grind your bones to dust
And with your blood and it I'll make a paste,
And of the paste a coffin I will rear
And make two pasties of your shameful heads,
And bid that strumpet, your unhallow'd dam,
Like to the earth swallow her own increase.
This is the feast that I have bid her to,
And this the banquet she shall surfeit on;
For worse than Philomel you used my daughter,
And worse than Progne I will be revenged:
And now prepare your throats. Lavinia, come,
Exeunt, bearing the dead bodies
Enter LUCIUS, MARCUS, and Goths, with AARON prisoner

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Uncle Marcus, since it is my father's mind
That I repair to Rome, I am content.

units.speaker: FIRST GOTH
units.text: And ours with thine, befall what fortune will.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Good uncle, take you in this barbarous Moor,
This ravenous tiger, this accursed devil;
Let him receive no sustenance, fetter him
Till he be brought unto the empress' face,
For testimony of her foul proceedings:
And see the ambush of our friends be strong;
I fear the emperor means no good to us.

units.speaker: AARON
units.text:
Some devil whisper curses in mine ear,
And prompt me, that my tongue may utter forth
The venomous malice of my swelling heart!

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Away, inhuman dog! unhallow'd slave!
Sirs, help our uncle to convey him in.
Exeunt Goths, with AARON. Flourish within

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: The trumpets show the emperor is at hand.
Enter SATURNINUS and TAMORA, with AEMILIUS, Tribunes, Senators, and others

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: What, hath the firmament more suns than one?

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text: What boots it thee to call thyself a sun?

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Rome's emperor, and nephew, break the parle;
These quarrels must be quietly debated.
The feast is ready, which the careful Titus
Hath ordain'd to an honourable end,
For peace, for love, for league, and good to Rome:
Please you, therefore, draw nigh, and take your places.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: Marcus, we will.
Hautboys sound. The Company sit down at table
Enter TITUS dressed like a Cook, LAVINIA veiled, Young LUCIUS, and others. TITUS places the dishes on the table

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
SATURNINUS
units.text: Why art thou thus attired, Andronicus?

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Because I would be sure to have all well,
To entertain your highness and your empress.

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: We are beholding to you, good Andronicus.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
An if your highness knew my heart, you were.
My lord the emperor, resolve me this:
Was it well done of rash Virginius
To slay his daughter with his own right hand,
Because she was enforced, stain'd, and deflower'd?

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: It was, Andronicus.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Your reason, mighty lord?

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text:
Because the girl should not survive her shame,
And by her presence still renew his sorrows.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
SATURNINUS
units.text: What hast thou done, unnatural and unkind?

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Kill'd her, for whom my tears have made me blind.
I am as woful as Virginius was,
And have a thousand times more cause than he
To do this outrage: and it now is done.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: What, was she ravish'd? tell who did the deed.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Will't please you eat? will't please your
highness feed?

units.speaker: TAMORA
units.text: Why hast thou slain thine only daughter thus?

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
SATURNINUS
units.text: Go fetch them hither to us presently.

units.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Why, there they are both, baked in that pie;
Whereof their mother daintily hath fed,
Eating the flesh that she herself hath bred.
'Tis true, 'tis true; witness my knife's sharp point.

units.speaker: SATURNINUS
units.text: Die, frantic wretch, for this accursed deed!

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Can the son's eye behold his father bleed?
There's meed for meed, death for a deadly deed!
Kills SATURNINUS. A great tumult. LUCIUS, MARCUS, and others go up into the balcony

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
You sad-faced men, people and sons of Rome,
By uproar sever'd, like a flight of fowl
Scatter'd by winds and high tempestuous gusts,
O, let me teach you how to knit again
This scatter'd corn into one mutual sheaf,
These broken limbs again into one body;
Lest Rome herself be bane unto herself,
And she whom mighty kingdoms court'sy to,
Like a forlorn and desperate castaway,
Do shameful execution on herself.
But if my frosty signs and chaps of age,
Grave witnesses of true experience,
Cannot induce you to attend my words,
[To Lucius]
Speak, Rome's dear friend, as erst our ancestor,
When with his solemn tongue he did discourse
To love-sick Dido's sad attending ear
The story of that baleful burning night
When subtle Greeks surprised King Priam's Troy,
Tell us what Sinon hath bewitch'd our ears,
Or who hath brought the fatal engine in
That gives our Troy, our Rome, the civil wound.
My heart is not compact of flint nor steel;
Nor can I utter all our bitter grief,
But floods of tears will drown my oratory,
And break my utterance, even in the time
When it should move you to attend me most,
Lending your kind commiseration.
Here is a captain, let him tell the tale;
Your hearts will throb and weep to hear him speak.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Then, noble auditory, be it known to you,
That cursed Chiron and Demetrius
Were they that murdered our emperor's brother;
And they it were that ravished our sister:
For their fell faults our brothers were beheaded;
Our father's tears despised, and basely cozen'd
Of that true hand that fought Rome's quarrel out,
And sent her enemies unto the grave.
Lastly, myself unkindly banished,
The gates shut on me, and turn'd weeping out,
To beg relief among Rome's enemies:
Who drown'd their enmity in my true tears.
And oped their arms to embrace me as a friend.
I am the turned forth, be it known to you,
That have preserved her welfare in my blood;
And from her bosom took the enemy's point,
Sheathing the steel in my adventurous body.
Alas, you know I am no vaunter, I;
My scars can witness, dumb although they are,
That my report is just and full of truth.
But, soft! methinks I do digress too much,
Citing my worthless praise: O, pardon me;
For when no friends are by, men praise themselves.

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: Now is my turn to speak. Behold this child:

units.speaker: AEMILIUS
units.text:
Come, come, thou reverend man of Rome,
And bring our emperor gently in thy hand,
Lucius our emperor; for well I know
The common voice do cry it shall be so.

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Lucius, all hail, Rome's royal emperor!

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Go, go into old Titus' sorrowful house,
[To Attendants]
And hither hale that misbelieving Moor,
To be adjudged some direful slaughtering death,
As punishment for his most wicked life.
Exeunt Attendants

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text: LUCIUS, MARCUS, and the others descend

units.speaker: ALL
units.text: Lucius, all hail, Rome's gracious governor!

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Thanks, gentle Romans: may I govern so,
To heal Rome's harms, and wipe away her woe!
But, gentle people, give me aim awhile,
For nature puts me to a heavy task:
Stand all aloof: but, uncle, draw you near,
To shed obsequious tears upon this trunk.
O, take this warm kiss on thy pale cold lips,

units.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
units.text:
Tear for tear, and loving kiss for kiss,
Thy brother Marcus tenders on thy lips:
O were the sum of these that I should pay
Countless and infinite, yet would I pay them!

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Come hither, boy; come, come, and learn of us
To melt in showers: thy grandsire loved thee well:
Many a time he danced thee on his knee,
Sung thee asleep, his loving breast thy pillow:
Many a matter hath he told to thee,
Meet and agreeing with thine infancy;
In that respect, then, like a loving child,
Shed yet some small drops from thy tender spring,
Because kind nature doth require it so:
Friends should associate friends in grief and woe:
Bid him farewell; commit him to the grave;
Do him that kindness, and take leave of him.

units.speaker: YOUNG LUCIUS
units.text:
O grandsire, grandsire! even with all my heart
Would I were dead, so you did live again!
O Lord, I cannot speak to him for weeping;
My tears will choke me, if I ope my mouth.
Re-enter Attendants with AARON

units.speaker: AEMILIUS
units.text:
You sad Andronici, have done with woes:
Give sentence on this execrable wretch,
That hath been breeder of these dire events.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
AARON
units.text:
O, why should wrath be mute, and fury dumb?
I am no baby, I, that with base prayers
I should repent the evils I have done:
Ten thousand worse than ever yet I did
Would I perform, if I might have my will;
If one good deed in all my life I did,
I do repent it from my very soul.

units.speaker: LUCIUS
units.text:
Some loving friends convey the emperor hence,
And give him burial in his father's grave:
My father and Lavinia shall forthwith
Be closed in our household's monument.
As for that heinous tiger, Tamora,
No funeral rite, nor man in mourning weeds,
No mournful bell shall ring her burial;
But throw her forth to beasts and birds of prey:
Her life was beast-like, and devoid of pity;
And, being so, shall have like want of pity.
See justice done on Aaron, that damn'd Moor,
By whom our heavy haps had their beginning:
Then, afterwards, to order well the state,
That like events may ne'er it ruinate.
Exeunt

sentences.text: The Tomb of the ANDRONICI appearing; the Tribunes and Senators aloft. Enter, below, from one side, SATURNINUS and his Followers; and, from the other side, BASSIANUS and his Followers; with drum and colours

The Tomb of the ANDRONICI appearing; the Tribunes and Senators aloft. Enter, below, from one side, SATURNINUS and his Followers; and, from the other side, BASSIANUS and his Followers; with drum and colours

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Noble patricians, patrons of my right,
Defend the justice of my cause with arms,
And, countrymen, my loving followers,
Plead my successive title with your swords:
I am his first-born son, that was the last
That wore the imperial diadem of Rome;
Then let my father's honours live in me,
Nor wrong mine age with this indignity.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:

That wore the imperial diadem of Rome;
Then let my father's honours live in me,
Nor wrong mine age with this indignity.

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
Romans, friends, followers, favorers of my right,
If ever Bassianus, Caesar's son,
Were gracious in the eyes of royal Rome,
Keep then this passage to the Capitol
And suffer not dishonour to approach
The imperial seat, to virtue consecrate,
To justice, continence and nobility;
But let desert in pure election shine,
And, Romans, fight for freedom in your choice.

Enter MARCUS ANDRONICUS, aloft, with the crown

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Princes, that strive by factions and by friends
Ambitiously for rule and empery,
Know that the people of Rome, for whom we stand
A special party, have, by common voice,
In election for the Roman empery,
Chosen Andronicus, surnamed Pius
For many good and great deserts to Rome:
A nobler man, a braver warrior,
Lives not this day within the city walls:
He by the senate is accit'd home
From weary wars against the barbarous Goths;
That, with his sons, a terror to our foes,
Hath yoked a nation strong, train'd up in arms.
Ten years are spent since first he undertook
This cause of Rome and chastised with arms
Our enemies' pride: five times he hath return'd
Bleeding to Rome, bearing his valiant sons
In coffins from the field;
And now at last, laden with horror's spoils,
Returns the good Andronicus to Rome,
Renowned Titus, flourishing in arms.
Let us entreat, by honour of his name,
Whom worthily you would have now succeed.
And in the Capitol and senate's right,
Whom you pretend to honour and adore,
That you withdraw you and abate your strength;
Dismiss your followers and, as suitors should,
Plead your deserts in peace and humbleness.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text: How fair the tribune speaks to calm my thoughts!

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
Marcus Andronicus, so I do ally
In thy uprightness and integrity,
And so I love and honour thee and thine,
Thy noble brother Titus and his sons,
And her to whom my thoughts are humbled all,
Gracious Lavinia, Rome's rich ornament,
That I will here dismiss my loving friends,
And to my fortunes and the people's favor
Commit my cause in balance to be weigh'd.

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
Marcus Andronicus, so I do ally
In thy uprightness and integrity,
And so I love and honour thee and thine,
Thy noble brother Titus and his sons,
And her to whom my thoughts are humbled all,
Gracious Lavinia, Rome's rich ornament,
That I will here dismiss my loving friends,
And to my fortunes and the people's favor
Commit my cause in balance to be weigh'd.
Exeunt the followers of BASSIANUS

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Friends, that have been thus forward in my right,
I thank you all and here dismiss you all,
And to the love and favor of my country
Commit myself, my person and the cause.
Exeunt the followers of SATURNINUS

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Rome, be as just and gracious unto me
As I am confident and kind to thee.
Open the gates, and let me in.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Rome, be as just and gracious unto me
As I am confident and kind to thee.
Open the gates, and let me in.

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text: Tribunes, and me, a poor competitor.
Flourish. SATURNINUS and BASSIANUS go up into the Capitol
Flourish. SATURNINUS and BASSIANUS go up into the Capitol
Enter a Captain

sentences.speaker: CAPTAIN
sentences.text:
Romans, make way: the good Andronicus.
Patron of virtue, Rome's best champion,
Successful in the battles that he fights,
With honour and with fortune is return'd
From where he circumscribed with his sword,
And brought to yoke, the enemies of Rome.

Drums and trumpets sounded. Enter MARTIUS and MUTIUS; After them, two Men bearing a coffin covered with black; then LUCIUS and QUINTUS. After them, TITUS ANDRONICUS; and then TAMORA, with ALARBUS, DEMETRIUS, CHIRON, AARON, and other Goths, prisoners; Soldiers and people following. The Bearers set down the coffin, and TITUS speaks

After them, TITUS ANDRONICUS; and then TAMORA, with ALARBUS, DEMETRIUS, CHIRON, AARON, and other Goths, prisoners; Soldiers and people following. The Bearers set down the coffin, and TITUS speaks

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Hail, Rome, victorious in thy mourning weeds!
Lo, as the bark, that hath discharged her fraught,
Returns with precious jading to the bay
From whence at first she weigh'd her anchorage,
Cometh Andronicus, bound with laurel boughs,
To re-salute his country with his tears,
Tears of true joy for his return to Rome.
Thou great defender of this Capitol,
Stand gracious to the rites that we intend!
Romans, of five and twenty valiant sons,
Half of the number that King Priam had,
Behold the poor remains, alive and dead!
These that survive let Rome reward with love;
These that I bring unto their latest home,
With burial amongst their ancestors:
Here Goths have given me leave to sheathe my sword.
Titus, unkind and careless of thine own,
Why suffer'st thou thy sons, unburied yet,
To hover on the dreadful shore of Styx?
Make way to lay them by their brethren.

Tears of true joy for his return to Rome.
Thou great defender of this Capitol,
Stand gracious to the rites that we intend!
Romans, of five and twenty valiant sons,
Half of the number that King Priam had,
Behold the poor remains, alive and dead!
These that survive let Rome reward with love;
These that I bring unto their latest home,
With burial amongst their ancestors:
Here Goths have given me leave to sheathe my sword.
Titus, unkind and careless of thine own,
Why suffer'st thou thy sons, unburied yet,
To hover on the dreadful shore of Styx?
Make way to lay them by their brethren.

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
Give us the proudest prisoner of the Goths,
That we may hew his limbs, and on a pile
Ad manes fratrum sacrifice his flesh,
Before this earthy prison of their bones;
That so the shadows be not unappeased,
Nor we disturb'd with prodigies on earth.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
I give him you, the noblest that survives,
The eldest son of this distressed queen.

sentences.speaker: TAMORA
sentences.text:
Stay, Roman brethren! Gracious conqueror,
Victorious Titus, rue the tears I shed,
A mother's tears in passion for her son:
And if thy sons were ever dear to thee,
O, think my son to be as dear to me!
Sufficeth not that we are brought to Rome,
To beautify thy triumphs and return,
Captive to thee and to thy Roman yoke,
But must my sons be slaughter'd in the streets,
For valiant doings in their country's cause?
O, if to fight for king and commonweal
Were piety in thine, it is in these.
Andronicus, stain not thy tomb with blood:
Wilt thou draw near the nature of the gods?
Draw near them then in being merciful:
Sweet mercy is nobility's true badge:
Thrice noble Titus, spare my first-born son.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Patient yourself, madam, and pardon me.
These are their brethren, whom you Goths beheld
Alive and dead, and for their brethren slain
Religiously they ask a sacrifice:
To this your son is mark'd, and die he must,
To appease their groaning shadows that are gone.

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
LUCIUS

sentences.text: Exeunt LUCIUS, QUINTUS, MARTIUS, and MUTIUS, with ALARBUS

sentences.speaker: TAMORA
sentences.text: O cruel, irreligious piety!

sentences.speaker: CHIRON
sentences.text: Was ever Scythia half so barbarous?

sentences.speaker: DEMETRIUS
sentences.text:
Oppose not Scythia to ambitious Rome.
Alarbus goes to rest; and we survive
To tremble under Titus' threatening looks.
Then, madam, stand resolved, but hope withal
The self-same gods that arm'd the Queen of Troy
With opportunity of sharp revenge
Upon the Thracian tyrant in his tent,
May favor Tamora, the Queen of Goths—
When Goths were Goths and Tamora was queen—
To quit the bloody wrongs upon her foes.

Re-enter LUCIUS, QUINTUS, MARTIUS and MUTIUS, with their swords bloody

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
See, lord and father, how we have perform'd
Our Roman rites: Alarbus' limbs are lopp'd,
And entrails feed the sacrificing fire,
Whose smoke, like incense, doth perfume the sky.
Remaineth nought, but to inter our brethren,
And with loud 'larums welcome them to Rome.

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:

And with loud 'larums welcome them to Rome.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Let it be so; and let Andronicus
Make this his latest farewell to their souls.
Trumpets sounded, and the coffin laid in the tomb

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
In peace and honour rest you here, my sons;
Rome's readiest champions, repose you here in rest,
Secure from worldly chances and mishaps!
Here lurks no treason, here no envy swells,
Here grow no damned grudges; here are no storms,
No noise, but silence and eternal sleep:
In peace and honour rest you here, my sons!

Enter LAVINIA

sentences.speaker: LAVINIA
sentences.text:
In peace and honour live Lord Titus long;
My noble lord and father, live in fame!
Lo, at this tomb my tributary tears
I render, for my brethren's obsequies;
And at thy feet I kneel, with tears of joy,
Shed on the earth, for thy return to Rome:
O, bless me here with thy victorious hand,
Whose fortunes Rome's best citizens applaud!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Kind Rome, that hast thus lovingly reserved
The cordial of mine age to glad my heart!
Lavinia, live; outlive thy father's days,
And fame's eternal date, for virtue's praise!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:

Enter, below, MARCUS ANDRONICUS and Tribunes; re-enter SATURNINUS and BASSIANUS, attended

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Long live Lord Titus, my beloved brother,
Gracious triumpher in the eyes of Rome!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Thanks, gentle tribune, noble brother Marcus.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
And welcome, nephews, from successful wars,
You that survive, and you that sleep in fame!
Fair lords, your fortunes are alike in all,
That in your country's service drew your swords:
But safer triumph is this funeral pomp,
That hath aspired to Solon's happiness
And triumphs over chance in honour's bed.
Titus Andronicus, the people of Rome,
Whose friend in justice thou hast ever been,
Send thee by me, their tribune and their trust,
This palliament of white and spotless hue;
And name thee in election for the empire,
With these our late-deceased emperor's sons:
Be candidatus then, and put it on,
And help to set a head on headless Rome.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
A better head her glorious body fits
Than his that shakes for age and feebleness:
What should I don this robe, and trouble you?
Be chosen with proclamations to-day,
To-morrow yield up rule, resign my life,
And set abroad new business for you all?
Rome, I have been thy soldier forty years,
And led my country's strength successfully,
And buried one and twenty valiant sons,
Knighted in field, slain manfully in arms,
In right and service of their noble country:
Give me a staff of honour for mine age,
But not a sceptre to control the world:
Upright he held it, lords, that held it last.

And set abroad new business for you all?
Rome, I have been thy soldier forty years,
And led my country's strength successfully,
And buried one and twenty valiant sons,
Knighted in field, slain manfully in arms,
In right and service of their noble country:
Give me a staff of honour for mine age,
But not a sceptre to control the world:
Upright he held it, lords, that held it last.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Titus, thou shalt obtain and ask the empery.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text: Proud and ambitious tribune, canst thou tell?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Patience, Prince Saturninus.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Romans, do me right:
Patricians, draw your swords: and sheathe them not
Till Saturninus be Rome's emperor.
Andronicus, would thou wert shipp'd to hell,
Rather than rob me of the people's hearts!

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Romans, do me right:
Patricians, draw your swords: and sheathe them not
Till Saturninus be Rome's emperor.
Andronicus, would thou wert shipp'd to hell,
Rather than rob me of the people's hearts!

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
Proud Saturnine, interrupter of the good
That noble-minded Titus means to thee!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Content thee, prince; I will restore to thee
The people's hearts, and wean them from themselves.

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
Andronicus, I do not flatter thee,
But honour thee, and will do till I die:
My faction if thou strengthen with thy friends,
I will most thankful be; and thanks to men
Of noble minds is honourable meed.

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
Andronicus, I do not flatter thee,
But honour thee, and will do till I die:
My faction if thou strengthen with thy friends,
I will most thankful be; and thanks to men
Of noble minds is honourable meed.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
People of Rome, and people's tribunes here,
I ask your voices and your suffrages:
Will you bestow them friendly on Andronicus?

sentences.speaker: TRIBUNES
sentences.text:
To gratify the good Andronicus,
And gratulate his safe return to Rome,
The people will accept whom he admits.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Tribunes, I thank you: and this suit I make,
That you create your emperor's eldest son,
Lord Saturnine; whose virtues will, I hope,
Reflect on Rome as Titan's rays on earth,
And ripen justice in this commonweal:
Then, if you will elect by my advice,
Crown him and say 'Long live our emperor!'

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
With voices and applause of every sort,
Patricians and plebeians, we create
Lord Saturninus Rome's great emperor,
And say 'Long live our Emperor Saturnine!'
A long flourish till they come down

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Titus Andronicus, for thy favors done
To us in our election this day,
I give thee thanks in part of thy deserts,
And will with deeds requite thy gentleness:
And, for an onset, Titus, to advance
Thy name and honourable family,
Lavinia will I make my empress,
Rome's royal mistress, mistress of my heart,
And in the sacred Pantheon her espouse:
Tell me, Andronicus, doth this motion please thee?

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Titus Andronicus, for thy favors done

I give thee thanks in part of thy deserts,
And will with deeds requite thy gentleness:
And, for an onset, Titus, to advance
Thy name and honourable family,
Lavinia will I make my empress,
Rome's royal mistress, mistress of my heart,
And in the sacred Pantheon her espouse:
Tell me, Andronicus, doth this motion please thee?

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Titus Andronicus, for thy favors done
To us in our election this day,
I give thee thanks in part of thy deserts,
And will with deeds requite thy gentleness:
And, for an onset, Titus, to advance

Lavinia will I make my empress,
Rome's royal mistress, mistress of my heart,
And in the sacred Pantheon her espouse:
Tell me, Andronicus, doth this motion please thee?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
It doth, my worthy lord; and in this match
I hold me highly honour'd of your grace:
And here in sight of Rome to Saturnine,
King and commander of our commonweal,
The wide world's emperor, do I consecrate
My sword, my chariot and my prisoners;
Presents well worthy Rome's imperial lord:
Receive them then, the tribute that I owe,
Mine honour's ensigns humbled at thy feet.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
It doth, my worthy lord; and in this match
I hold me highly honour'd of your grace:
And here in sight of Rome to Saturnine,
King and commander of our commonweal,
The wide world's emperor, do I consecrate
My sword, my chariot and my prisoners;
Presents well worthy Rome's imperial lord:
Receive them then, the tribute that I owe,
Mine honour's ensigns humbled at thy feet.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Thanks, noble Titus, father of my life!
How proud I am of thee and of thy gifts
Rome shall record, and when I do forget
The least of these unspeakable deserts,
Romans, forget your fealty to me.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Thanks, noble Titus, father of my life!
How proud I am of thee and of thy gifts
Rome shall record, and when I do forget
The least of these unspeakable deserts,
Romans, forget your fealty to me.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
[To TAMORA] Now, madam, are you prisoner to
an emperor;
To him that, for your honour and your state,
Will use you nobly and your followers.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
A goodly lady, trust me; of the hue
That I would choose, were I to choose anew.
Clear up, fair queen, that cloudy countenance:
Though chance of war hath wrought this change of cheer,
Thou comest not to be made a scorn in Rome:
Princely shall be thy usage every way.
Rest on my word, and let not discontent
Daunt all your hopes: madam, he comforts you
Can make you greater than the Queen of Goths.
Lavinia, you are not displeased with this?

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LAVINIA
sentences.text:
Not I, my lord; sith true nobility
Warrants these words in princely courtesy.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
SATURNINUS
SATURNINUS

sentences.text: Flourish. SATURNINUS courts TAMORA in dumb show
Flourish. SATURNINUS courts TAMORA in dumb show

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text: Lord Titus, by your leave, this maid is mine.

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text: Lord Titus, by your leave, this maid is mine.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: How, sir! are you in earnest then, my lord?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: How, sir! are you in earnest then, my lord?

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
Ay, noble Titus; and resolved withal
To do myself this reason and this right.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
'Suum cuique' is our Roman justice:
This prince in justice seizeth but his own.

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text: And that he will, and shall, if Lucius live.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, avaunt! Where is the emperor's guard?
Treason, my lord! Lavinia is surprised!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, avaunt! Where is the emperor's guard?
Treason, my lord! Lavinia is surprised!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, avaunt! Where is the emperor's guard?
Treason, my lord! Lavinia is surprised!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, avaunt! Where is the emperor's guard?
Treason, my lord! Lavinia is surprised!

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text: Surprised! by whom?

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text: Surprised! by whom?

sentences.speaker: BASSIANUS
sentences.text:
By him that justly may
Bear his betroth'd from all the world away.
Exeunt BASSIANUS and MARCUS with LAVINIA

sentences.speaker: MUTIUS
sentences.text:
Brothers, help to convey her hence away,
And with my sword I'll keep this door safe.
Exeunt LUCIUS, QUINTUS, and MARTIUS

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Follow, my lord, and I'll soon bring her back.

sentences.speaker: MUTIUS
sentences.text: My lord, you pass not here.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
What, villain boy!
Barr'st me my way in Rome?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
What, villain boy!
Barr'st me my way in Rome?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
What, villain boy!
Barr'st me my way in Rome?

sentences.speaker: MUTIUS
sentences.text: Help, Lucius, help!
Dies
During the fray, SATURNINUS, TAMORA, DEMETRIUS, CHIRON and AARON go out and re-enter, above
Re-enter LUCIUS

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
My lord, you are unjust, and, more than so,
In wrongful quarrel you have slain your son.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
TITUS ANDRONICUS
LUCIUS
sentences.text:
Dead, if you will; but not to be his wife,
That is another's lawful promised love.

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
Dead, if you will; but not to be his wife,
That is another's lawful promised love.
Exit

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
No, Titus, no; the emperor needs her not,
Nor her, nor thee, nor any of thy stock:
I'll trust, by leisure, him that mocks me once;
Thee never, nor thy traitorous haughty sons,
Confederates all thus to dishonour me.
Was there none else in Rome to make a stale,
But Saturnine? Full well, Andronicus,
Agree these deeds with that proud brag of thine,
That said'st I begg'd the empire at thy hands.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: O monstrous! what reproachful words are these?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: O monstrous! what reproachful words are these?

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
But go thy ways; go, give that changing piece
To him that flourish'd for her with his sword
A valiant son-in-law thou shalt enjoy;
One fit to bandy with thy lawless sons,
To ruffle in the commonwealth of Rome.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
But go thy ways; go, give that changing piece
To him that flourish'd for her with his sword
A valiant son-in-law thou shalt enjoy;
One fit to bandy with thy lawless sons,
To ruffle in the commonwealth of Rome.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: These words are razors to my wounded heart.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
And therefore, lovely Tamora, queen of Goths,
That like the stately Phoebe 'mongst her nymphs
Dost overshine the gallant'st dames of Rome,
If thou be pleased with this my sudden choice,
Behold, I choose thee, Tamora, for my bride,
And will create thee empress of Rome,
Speak, Queen of Goths, dost thou applaud my choice?
And here I swear by all the Roman gods,
Sith priest and holy water are so near
And tapers burn so bright and every thing
In readiness for Hymenaeus stand,
I will not re-salute the streets of Rome,
Or climb my palace, till from forth this place
I lead espoused my bride along with me.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: TAMORA
sentences.text:
And here, in sight of heaven, to Rome I swear,
If Saturnine advance the Queen of Goths,
She will a handmaid be to his desires,
A loving nurse, a mother to his youth.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Ascend, fair queen, Pantheon. Lords, accompany
Your noble emperor and his lovely bride,
Sent by the heavens for Prince Saturnine,
Whose wisdom hath her fortune conquered:
There shall we consummate our spousal rites.

sentences.speaker: SATURNINUS
sentences.text:
Ascend, fair queen, Pantheon. Lords, accompany
Your noble emperor and his lovely bride,
Sent by the heavens for Prince Saturnine,
Whose wisdom hath her fortune conquered:
There shall we consummate our spousal rites.
Exeunt all but TITUS

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
I am not bid to wait upon this bride.
Titus, when wert thou wont to walk alone,
Dishonour'd thus, and challenged of wrongs?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
I am not bid to wait upon this bride.
Titus, when wert thou wont to walk alone,
Dishonour'd thus, and challenged of wrongs?
Re-enter MARCUS, LUCIUS, QUINTUS, and MARTIUS

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
O Titus, see, O, see what thou hast done!
In a bad quarrel slain a virtuous son.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
O Titus, see, O, see what thou hast done!
In a bad quarrel slain a virtuous son.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
No, foolish tribune, no; no son of mine,
Nor thou, nor these, confederates in the deed
That hath dishonour'd all our family;
Unworthy brother, and unworthy sons!

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
No, foolish tribune, no; no son of mine,
Nor thou, nor these, confederates in the deed
That hath dishonour'd all our family;
Unworthy brother, and unworthy sons!

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
But let us give him burial, as becomes;
Give Mutius burial with our brethren.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, away! he rests not in this tomb:
This monument five hundred years hath stood,
Which I have sumptuously re-edified:
Here none but soldiers and Rome's servitors
Repose in fame; none basely slain in brawls:
Bury him where you can; he comes not here.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, away! he rests not in this tomb:
This monument five hundred years hath stood,
Which I have sumptuously re-edified:
Here none but soldiers and Rome's servitors
Repose in fame; none basely slain in brawls:
Bury him where you can; he comes not here.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Traitors, away! he rests not in this tomb:
This monument five hundred years hath stood,
Which I have sumptuously re-edified:
Here none but soldiers and Rome's servitors
Repose in fame; none basely slain in brawls:
Bury him where you can; he comes not here.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
My lord, this is impiety in you:
My nephew Mutius' deeds do plead for him
He must be buried with his brethren.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
My lord, this is impiety in you:
My nephew Mutius' deeds do plead for him
He must be buried with his brethren.

sentences.speaker: MARTIUS
sentences.text: And shall, or him we will accompany.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
'And shall!' what villain was it that spake
that word?

sentences.speaker: QUINTUS
sentences.text: He that would vouch it in any place but here.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: What, would you bury him in my despite?

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
No, noble Titus, but entreat of thee
To pardon Mutius and to bury him.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Marcus, even thou hast struck upon my crest,
And, with these boys, mine honour thou hast wounded:
My foes I do repute you every one;
So, trouble me no more, but get you gone.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Marcus, even thou hast struck upon my crest,
And, with these boys, mine honour thou hast wounded:
My foes I do repute you every one;
So, trouble me no more, but get you gone.

sentences.speaker: MARTIUS
sentences.text: He is not with himself; let us withdraw.

sentences.speaker: QUINTUS
sentences.text: Not I, till Mutius' bones be buried.
MARCUS and the Sons of TITUS kneel

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Brother, for in that name doth nature plead,—

sentences.speaker: QUINTUS
sentences.text: Father, and in that name doth nature speak,—

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Speak thou no more, if all the rest will speed.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text: Renowned Titus, more than half my soul,—

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text: Dear father, soul and substance of us all,—

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Suffer thy brother Marcus to inter
His noble nephew here in virtue's nest,
That died in honour and Lavinia's cause.
Thou art a Roman; be not barbarous:
The Greeks upon advice did bury Ajax
That slew himself; and wise Laertes' son
Did graciously plead for his funerals:
Let not young Mutius, then, that was thy joy
Be barr'd his entrance here.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Suffer thy brother Marcus to inter

That died in honour and Lavinia's cause.
Thou art a Roman; be not barbarous:

That slew himself; and wise Laertes' son
Did graciously plead for his funerals:
Let not young Mutius, then, that was thy joy
Be barr'd his entrance here.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Rise, Marcus, rise.
The dismall'st day is this that e'er I saw,
To be dishonour'd by my sons in Rome!
Well, bury him, and bury me the next.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Rise, Marcus, rise.
The dismall'st day is this that e'er I saw,
To be dishonour'd by my sons in Rome!
Well, bury him, and bury me the next.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Rise, Marcus, rise.
The dismall'st day is this that e'er I saw,
To be dishonour'd by my sons in Rome!
Well, bury him, and bury me the next.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
Rise, Marcus, rise.
The dismall'st day is this that e'er I saw,

Well, bury him, and bury me the next.

sentences.speaker: LUCIUS
sentences.text:
There lie thy bones, sweet Mutius, with thy friends,
Till we with trophies do adorn thy tomb.

sentences.speaker: ALL
sentences.text:
[Kneeling] No man shed tears for noble Mutius;
He lives in fame that died in virtue's cause.

sentences.speaker: MARCUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
My lord, to step out of these dreary dumps,
How comes it that the subtle Queen of Goths
Is of a sudden thus advanced in Rome?

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:
I know not, Marcus; but I know it is,
Whether by device or no, the heavens can tell:
Is she not then beholding to the man
That brought her for this high good turn so far?
Yes, and will nobly him remunerate.

sentences.speaker: TITUS ANDRONICUS
sentences.text:

That brought her for this high good turn so far?
Yes, and will nobly him remunerate.

Whether by device or no, the heavens can tell:
Is she not then beholding to the man
That brought her for this high good turn so far?
Yes, and will nobly him remunerate.
Flourish. Re-enter, from one side, SATURNINUS attended, TAMORA, DEMETRIUS, CHIRON and AARON; from the other, BASSIANUS, LAVINIA, and others
